The first time I saw her, she was sitting on the beach, her back against a
rock, her eyes fixed on the sea.  She was a girl of about twenty, with
long, fair hair, and a white dress that was torn and stained.  She was
thin, and her skin was pale, but she had a beauty that was almost
painful.  She was so still, so quiet, that I thought she was dead.  I
walked closer, and she looked up at me.  She was not dead, but she was
dying.  She was dying of hunger, and of thirst, and of the cold.
